flow_page_title: Process Flow of Explosion Effect Simulation and Modeling Based on TNT Equivalence
thresholds_page_title: Overpressure thresholds based on TNT equivalent
page_subtitle: Chemical Engineering, Bandung Institute of Technology
s1_details:
The user begins the simulation by providing three primary inputs:
  • Material Selection: Choose from Ammonium Nitrate (AN), Propane (LPG), or Hydrogen (H₂).
  • Volume (m³): The volume of the selected material.
  • Distance (m): The distance from the explosion's origin to the point of interest.
s2_details:
After selecting a material, its chemical properties from literature are automatically loaded. The user can review or modify these values:
  • Density (ρ): ... kg/m³ (Default and editable value)
  • ΔHexplosion: ... kJ/kg (Default and editable value)
  • Ambient Pressure (Pa): 101.325 kPa (Default 101.325)
  • ηE Explosion Efficiency: ... (Default)
  • ETNT: ... kJ/kg (Default and editable value)
s3_alonso:
(Po) Alonso (kPa):
If Ze < 10 → (1.13e6 * Ze⁻²·⁰¹) / 1000
If Ze ≥ 10 → (1.83e5 * Ze⁻¹·¹⁶) / 1000
s4_details:
The application validates the calculated `Ze` value. A warning is issued if the value is outside the recommended range for each model.
  • Crowl: 0.01 ≤ Ze ≤ 100
  • Alonso: 1 ≤ Ze ≤ 200
  • Sadovski: 1 ≤ Ze ≤ 15
s5_details:
The calculated `Po` values are used to:
  • Estimate Damage to Structures & Equipment.
  • Estimate Injury Consequences.
  • Plot the (Ze, Ps) point on the Ps vs. Ze Chart.
s6_subtitle: User saves results to the Log
s6_details: The user can save the complete set of inputs and outputs as a 'Node' in the log table for comparison.
s7_details:
Data from all 'Nodes' in the log are used to automatically generate:
  • Overpressure vs. Distance Chart.
  • Explosion Contour Map.
sr1_details: The user starts by importing a simulation file (.csv) or the application automatically loads the last simulation data from browser storage. The entire dataset is loaded into the Simulation Log table.
sr2_details: Based on the imported or loaded data, the 'Overpressure vs. Distance Chart' and the 'Explosion Contour Map' are immediately plotted. Data from Node 1 is displayed by default.
sr3_subtitle: User selects a 'Node' from the table
sr3_details: When the user clicks a specific 'Node' row within the log table...
sr4_details: The application instantly updates the 'Damage Estimation' and 'Injury Estimation' panels with the values from the selected Node. The corresponding point on the Ps vs. Ze Chart is also highlighted.
sr5_details: As the final step of the reverse workflow, the initial input fields (Material, Volume, Distance) and Properties are populated with data from the selected Node, allowing the user to review or modify an existing simulation.

footer_dedication: This application is dedicated to my parents for their persistent support and patient guidance throughout my JavaScript journey. It is also presented to the academic community at Bandung Institute of Technology as a contribution towards inspiring further advancements in JavaScript technology.
footer_disclaimer: This application is part of my thesis research concerning the evaluation of explosion consequences in relation to distance for various chemical substances (AN & H2). Its purpose is purely academic, intended as a preliminary assessment for establishing safe distances. I sincerely welcome any constructive feedback and criticism to enrich this study, both from the perspective of chemical engineering and the implementation of JavaScript technology.
footer_disclaimer2: This work is part of my thesis research on evaluating the consequences of explosions relative to distance for various chemical materials (AN and H₂). The objective is purely academic, serving as an initial estimation for determining safe distances in process facilities. I sincerely appreciate any feedback or constructive criticism to enhance this study, whether from a chemical engineering perspective or in terms of digital and interactive data presentation.
page_title: Simulation & Modeling of Explosion Effects (TNT Equivalence)
app_title: Comparative Simulation and Modeling of Explosion Consequences based on TNT Equivalent
estimation_damage_title: Estimation of Damage to Structures and Process Equipment
eq_panel_placeholder: Select a material to display the reaction equation.
chart_noscript: Please enable JavaScript to display the chart. Reference: G. F. Kinney & K. J. Graham (1985).
chart_noscript_generic: Please enable JavaScript to display the chart.
chart_note_source: Source: G. F. Kinney & K. J. Graham, Explosive Shocks in Air, Springer-Verlag, 1985.
log_footnote: Click "Save" to log the calculation results. The last 10 entries will be stored in your browser.

status_success: The calculation has been completed successfully.
status_error_range: Ensure that the property is entered within the appropriate range.
status_error_invalid_ze: The calculation outcome is invalid (Ze is non-positive).
status_log_empty_export: No log data are available for export.
status_import_success: Log data from CSV file imported successfully!
status_import_error: The file could not be imported:
status_import_error_empty: CSV file is invalid or empty.
status_import_error_no_data: No valid data could be retrieved from the CSV file.
status_log_saved: Log entry saved successfully to browser storage.
error_fan_range: fAN is outside the valid range (0.300–0.550).
alonso_warning: Warning: Result is extrapolated beyond the valid Alonso model range (1 ≤ ze ≤ 200).
sadovski_warning: Warning: Result is extrapolated beyond the valid Sadovski model range (1 ≤ ze ≤ 15).
crowl_warning: Warning: Result is extrapolated beyond the valid Crowl model range (1 ≤ Ze ≤ 100).
ai_report_prompt_label: Enter a case study scenario (e.g., "LPG tank explosion"):

pe_dmg_1: No significant damage to process equipment is recorded.
pe_dmg_2: Windows and gauges in both steel- and concrete-roof control houses break, and louvers on the cooling tower fall off [3].
pe_dmg_3: The steel-roof control house roof collapses and damages the switchgear, instruments in the concrete-roof control house are damaged, and the cone-roof tank roof collapses [3].
pe_dmg_4: The steel-roof control house roof collapses, the frame of the concrete-roof control house deforms, and windows and gauges in the instrument cubicle break [3].
pe_dmg_5: The concrete-roof control house roof collapses, internal parts of the cooling tower are damaged, fire-heater bricks crack, windows and gauges of the chemical reactor break, and the filter suffers debris/missile damage [3].
pe_dmg_6: The fire heater is displaced and connected pipes break [3].
pe_dmg_7: Cone-roof and floating-roof tanks uplift and become tilted, power lines in the instrument cubicle are severed, controls are damaged, and the regenerator moves so that attached piping breaks [3].
pe_dmg_8: Block walls of both steel- and concrete-roof control houses fall, the cooling-tower frame collapses, the cracking reactor moves and attached piping breaks, and pine-support frames deform [3].
pe_dmg_9: The chemical reactor moves and its connected piping breaks [3].
pe_dmg_10: Internal parts of the filter are damaged, the gas-meter case is damaged, and the utilities electronic transformer suffers debris/missile damage [3].
pe_dmg_11: The fire heater overturns or is destroyed, the regenerator moves and piping breaks, structural frames deform, debris from the electric motor causes missile damage, and the blower case is damaged [3].
pe_dmg_13: The instrument cubicle overturns or is destroyed, piping on pine supports breaks and the frame collapses, horizontal pressure-vessel frames deform, several units move with broken pipes, and the gas-regulator utilities unit moves and its piping breaks [3].
pe_dmg_14: Cone-roof and floating-roof tanks uplift with large tilt, the chemical-reactor frame deforms, and the extraction column moves so that connected piping breaks [3].
pe_dmg_15: The cracking reactor moves and piping breaks, and the fractionation column overturns or is destroyed [3].
pe_dmg_16: The regenerator overturns or is destroyed, the utilities electronic transformer moves and piping breaks, and both the steam turbine and heat exchanger move so that their connected piping breaks [3].
pe_dmg_17: The spherical tank moves and its connected pipes break [3].
pe_dmg_18: The chemical reactor overturns or is destroyed, the electric motor moves and piping breaks, and the horizontal pressure vessel and heat exchanger overturn or are destroyed [3].
pe_dmg_19: The filter uplifts and is heavily tilted [3].
pe_dmg_20: The utilities electronic transformer and the blower overturn or are destroyed, the gas regulator controls and case are damaged, and the extraction column shifts on its foundation [3].
pe_dmg_21: The filter, cracking reactor, and extraction column overturn or are destroyed, steam-turbine controls are damaged, and the vertical pressure vessel and pump move with broken pipes [3].
pe_dmg_22: Steam-turbine piping breaks, the spherical tank moves and piping breaks, and the vertical pressure vessel overturns or is destroyed [3].
pe_dmg_23: The spherical tank overturns or is destroyed and the pump shifts on its foundation [3].
pe_dmg_24: The floating-roof tank roof collapses, the electric motor moves on its foundation, and the steam turbine moves on its foundation [3].
bld_dmg_pt_1: Annoying noise (137 dB if low frequency, 10–15 Hz) [2].
bld_dmg_pt_2: Breakage of large windows previously under stress [2].
bld_dmg_pt_3: Loud noise (143 dB), sonic boom, glass failure [2].
bld_dmg_pt_4: Small windows break due to pressure [2].
bld_dmg_pt_6: Safe distance, 95% probability of no serious damage below this level, projectile limit, minor ceiling damage, 10% window breakage [2].
bld_dmg_pt_10: Slight distortion of coated steel frames [2].
bld_dmg_pt_11: Partial collapse of roof and walls [2].
bld_dmg_pt_12: Lower limit of serious structural damage [2].
bld_dmg_pt_14: Heavy machinery (3,000 lb) slightly damaged in industrial buildings; steel frames distorted or detached from foundation [2].
bld_dmg_pt_16: Utility poles break; 40,000 lb hydraulic equipment slightly damaged [2].
bld_dmg_pt_17: 8–12 inch thick unreinforced brick panels fail from shear or bending [2].
bld_dmg_pt_19: Fully loaded train cars totally destroyed [2].
bld_dmg_pt_20: Likely total damage to structures; 7,000 lb equipment displaced and severely damaged; 12,000 lb machinery still present [2].
bld_dmg_rg_2: Minor glass breakage and light architectural damage such as displacement of roof tiles and falling plaster [1].
bld_dmg_rg_3: Widespread glass shattering and significant damage to non-structural elements like doors, windows, and roofs [1].
bld_dmg_rg_4: Collapse of unreinforced concrete walls or cement blocks [2].
bld_dmg_rg_5: Frame-less steel panels destroyed; oil storage tanks ruptured (20.7 kPa - ≤25 kPa) [2].
bld_dmg_rg_6: Frame-less steel panels destroyed; oil storage tanks ruptured (25 kPa - 27.6 kPa) [2].
bld_dmg_rg_7: Extensive damage to non-structural elements such as brick facades, roofs, and ceilings [1].
bld_dmg_rg_8: Near-total destruction of residential houses (34.5 kPa - ≤40 kPa) [2].
bld_dmg_rg_9: Near-total destruction of residential houses (40 kPa - 48.2 kPa) [2].
bld_dmg_rg_10: Heavy damage to non-structural elements leading to ceiling collapse [1].
bld_dmg_rg_11: 8–12 inch thick unreinforced brick panels fail from shear or bending (48.2 kPa - ≤55 kPa) [2].
bld_dmg_rg_12: 8–12 inch thick unreinforced brick panels fail from shear or bending (55 kPa - 55.1 kPa) [2].
bld_dmg_rg_13: Partial collapse of non-structural elements and significant damage to concrete columns [1].
bld_dmg_rg_14: Near-total collapse of all building elements, including heavy damage to load-bearing concrete columns [1].
inj_1_p: No serious injuries; eardrum rupture is not significant.
inj_1_s: Glass cracking can begin around 1–1.5 kPa [4]; widespread breakage typically 2–9 kPa [1].
inj_1_c: Primary injury is unlikely, but secondary injury risk from glass fragments remains.
inj_2_s: Moderate structure damage may cause ceiling sections to fall and small objects to drop [2].
inj_2_c: Combination of minor contusion and secondary injuries from fragments/debris.
inj_3_p: Moderate contusion [1]; eardrum rupture probability 50% around 110 kPa [4].
inj_3_s: Wider structural damage creates flying debris [2], which can cause fractures.
inj_3_c: Injury severity rises due to the combined effects of the blast wave and flying debris.
inj_4_p: Serious internal contusions and possibly fatal [1]; risk of blast-lung depends on positive-phase duration with overpressure 70 kPa at 50 ms or 140–200 kPa at 3 ms [4].
inj_4_s: Many buildings heavily damaged; some may be totally destroyed [2], creating entrapment under rubble.
inj_4_c: Concurrence of internal injuries and entrapment is potentially fatal.
inj_5_p: High mortality for unprotected persons, but thresholds depend on positive-phase duration: 1% lethality 190 kPa at 50 ms or 400–500 kPa at 3 ms [4].
inj_5_s: Total building collapse; large debris moving rapidly [2].
inj_5_c: Fatality risk escalates where both overpressure and duration exceed thresholds.
table1_footer: References: Crowl & Louvar (2011), citing V. J. Clancey, “Diagnostic Features of Explosion Damage,” paper presented at the Sixth International Meeting of Forensic Sciences (Edinburgh, 1972) and Wang et al. (2023).
table2_footer: References: Crowl & Louvar (2011); Jeremić & Bajić (2006); Wang et al. (2023).
table3_footer: References: CCPS (2000), citing V. J. Clancey, “Diagnostic Features of Explosion Damage,” paper presented at the Sixth International Meeting of Forensic Sciences (Edinburgh, 1972).
